The programme supports high-impact, research-driven innovations aligned with ESA Phi-Lab Sweden.

science.title: Stand composition shapes canopy structure, competition, and soil properties in virgin oriental beech forests
science.description: Forest stand composition influences ecosystem structure and functioning, yet its integrated effects on canopy architecture, competition intensity, and soil processes are rarely evaluated under undisturbed conditions that provide essential silvicultural reference states. The virgin oriental beech forests of the Hyrcanian region in northern Iran represent one of the last remaining temperate broadleaf forest systems worldwide that have developed without logging, grazing, or silvicultural intervention, providing a rare natural reference for examining coupled above- and belowground processes. We compared pure and mixed beech stands by quantifying canopy structural attributes, competition indices, and key organic and mineral soil properties. Pure stands exhibited larger trees, stronger vertical canopy organization, and significantly higher competition intensity, whereas mixed stands supported greater microbial biomass and respiration in the mineral soil despite lower competitive pressure. Correlation and redundancy analyses revealed strong linkages between canopy structure, competition intensity, and soil properties, with clear multivariate separation between pure and mixed stands. These findings demonstrate that stand composition reorganizes the coupling between canopy structure, competition intensity, and soil processes, highlighting the importance of integrating above- and belowground components to understand ecosystem functioning in undisturbed forests. By leveraging one of the few remaining virgin temperate beech forest landscapes, this study provides a basis for targeted silvicultural and restoration strategies by identifying when mixed-species stands enhance soil microbial functioning and when pure beech stands promote larger tree development and greater vertical canopy stratification.

science.title: Researchers report interfacial Ising superconductivity in a graphene‑capped gallium trilayer
science.description: Researchers from Penn State, University of Oxford, Zhejiang University, Diamond Light Source and the University of North Texas have demonstrated a new route to Ising‑type superconductivity in a lightweight, low‑dimensional material by combining quantum confinement with strong interfacial hybridization. Using plasma‑free confinement epitaxy aided by a carbon buffer layer, they synthesized a gallium trilayer sandwiched between graphene and a 6H‑SiC(0001) substrate, creating a two‑dimensional superconducting channel where Cooper pairs are stabilized against in‑plane magnetic fields well beyond the Pauli paramagnetic limit.In this structure, three atomic layers of gallium are confined between a silicon carbide (6H‑SiC) substrate below and a graphene capping layer above. The graphene both protects the gallium from oxidation and defines the top interface, while the SiC substrate provides a rigid template and a source of strong interfacial coupling. Electrical transport measurements show that the system becomes superconducting at low temperatures, with an in‑plane upper critical magnetic field of about 21.98 T at 400 mK, which is approximately 3.38 times the conventional Pauli paramagnetic limit for this material. The persistence of superconductivity at such high in‑plane fields arises from Ising‑type superconductivity, in which the spin of the electrons is effectively locked perpendicular to the plane of the gallium layer. This “spin‑locking” protects Cooper pairs from being broken by magnetic fields parallel to the surface, in contrast to conventional superconductors whose upper critical fields are capped near the Pauli limit. Angle‑resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) and theoretical calculations reveal split Fermi surfaces with Ising‑type spin textures at the K and K′ valleys of the confined gallium layer, a signature of strong hybridization between gallium orbitals and those of the underlying SiC substrate.Typically, Ising‑type superconductivity is associated with superconductors containing heavy elements that naturally host strong spin-orbit coupling. In this work, however, the effect emerges in a light‑element system - gallium - through interface‑driven band‑structure engineering rather than intrinsic atomic properties. The combination of quantum confinement (from the three‑atomic‑layer geometry) and interfacial hybridization (with the SiC substrate) reshapes the Cooper‑pair wavefunctions, leading to unconventional superconducting states similar to those seen in heavy‑element compounds.
